retrieved_docs.title: Pin Index Safety System  -- Issues of Concern -- Pin Index Configurations for Commonly Used Medical Gases
retrieved_docs.content: These numbers describe the location of the pins in millimeters from the edge of the yoke, [3] with pin #1 being the farthest to the right when looking at the system straight on. The first pin is located 30° from horizontal on the gas tank yoke, and each subsequent pin is located 12° further down, creating a semicircle. These numbers and pin placements are standardized across countries.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Infection Control -- Clinical Significance -- Common Methods of Sterilization
retrieved_docs.content: Steam at 121 degrees C for 20 to 30 minutes or 134 degrees C for 2 to 10 minutes. Advantages – good penetration Precautions – carbon steel corrodes, damage to plastic and rubber items, packs wet after the cycle, hard water spots instruments.Unsaturated chemical vapor – 20 minutes at 134 C Unsaturated chemical vapor – 20 minutes at 134 C Advantages – no corrosion of carbon steel, packs are dry after cycle Precautions – may damage plastic and rubber items. Dry heat (Oven Type) – 1 to 2 hours at 160 C Advantages – no corrosion of carbon steel, packs dry after the cycle Precautions – may damage plastic and rubber items, do not open door during the cycle. Dry heat (rapid heat transfer type) 6 to 12 minutes at 191 C Advantages – short cycle Precautions – may damage plastic and rubber items. Glass bead sterilizers  : Used  in endodontics to sterilize reamers, files though their efficacy is debatable when compared with autoclaving . [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pin Index Safety System  -- Issues of Concern -- Pin Index Configurations for Commonly Used Medical Gases
retrieved_docs.content: Each PISS has 6 possible pin placement sites, depending on the gas within the cylinder. The pins protrude from the gas tank yokes and fit corresponding holes in the cylinder valves, providing a foolproof way to connect the gas to the appropriate valve. Two pins are placed per cylinder, allowing for multiple medically used gases that have been added to the standard formats. The following are examples of pin locations for common medical gases: Oxygen: 2, 5 Nitro oxide: 3, 5 Air: 1, 5 (see Image. Pin Index Safety System) Carbon dioxide: 1, 6 Nitrogen: 1, 4

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: To open the instrument trays, ensure there is a plastic lock that identifies an indicator change, a label with the Julian date and load number, and make sure it is the correct set for the procedure. Look at the outside container for moisture or condensation; if there is moisture, do not use it and retrieve another set. If there is no condensation, proceed by grasping the latches and flipping them open, automatically breaking the plastic locks. Lift the lid straight up and step back before flipping the lid over; this ensures that dust or particles don't fall into the tray. Once you flip the lid over, check for moisture and remove the paper filter. To check the filter for any holes, lift it toward a light to ensure no pin-size holes. If the filter looks good, carefully look into the pan for an indicator; do not lean over the pan. If you cannot see an indicator, look more closely after being gowned and gloved. Until you are scrubbed in, the instruments are complete; once you are gowned and gloved, carefully pick up the inside basket and hold it in front of you until the circulating nurse checks the bottom filter and the bottom of the tray for moisture. This is also an excellent time to find the inside indicator. If you cannot find an indicator, do not use the tray. Once the nurse verifies the instrument tray and you see an indicator, you can place the instruments on the back table.

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Counted Scrub Method
retrieved_docs.content: You scrub 1 hand and then proceed to the other hand and arm

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Counted Scrub Method
retrieved_docs.content: Each finger is divided into 4 sides; each side performs ten strokes for a total of 40 strokes for each finger

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Counted Scrub Method
retrieved_docs.content: Once the fingers are completed, proceed to the palm for 30 strokes and then the dorsal side of the hand for 30 strokes

retrieved_docs.title: Sterile Technique -- Technique or Treatment -- The Counted Scrub Method
retrieved_docs.content: The forearm is next, and it is divided into 4 planes (planes are from the wrist to 2 inches above the elbow); each plane is scrubbed separately, performed in a circular motion for ten strokes each

retrieved_docs.title: Gas Cylinders -- Issues of Concern -- Safe Usage
retrieved_docs.content: A cylinder should be inspected for malfunctions and defects before use. Full cylinders are usually placed with a tamper-evident seal. This is generally a tear-off seal on the valve outlet and is removed before use. Proper cylinder inspection includes the outlet, pin index safety system, and pressure relief device. The valve outlet should be cleaned before use. Only use cylinders marked with DOT (Department of Transportation) or ICC (Interstate Commerce Commission). In Canada, cylinders may be marked with BTC (Board of Transportation Commissioners) or CTC (Canadian Transport Commission). A cylinder should be connected to a regulator to reduce the compressed, stored pressure to a working, usable pressure. Inspect the regulator for signs of damage or foreign materials. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Pin Index Safety System  -- Issues of Concern -- Improvements of the Pin Index Safety System
retrieved_docs.content: A second case report in 1995 documented an incident in which a woman undergoing a laparoscopic cholecystectomy sustained an intra-abdominal fire after a trocar caught fire, leading to the ignition of the insufflating gas within the abdomen. Post-incident analysis revealed that the company providing the gas had a policy that canisters with greater than 7% CO2 were to be fitted with a CO2 pin index; however, this particular canister had a green stripe around the top to identify it as having a majority of O 2 , not CO 2 . This specific canister in the operating room was 14% CO 2 and 86% O 2 , leading to the operating room fire. This incident highlighted the potential for human error in the PISS and the potential downfalls in labeling cylinders with mixed gases. [2] Subsequently, efforts were made to distinguish O 2 from CO 2 and improve oversight.

retrieved_docs.title: Cobalt Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other potential occupational exposures can occur in the maintenance of hard-metal blades [18] and diamond polishing. [19] The resultant inhalation of aerosolized dissolved and ionized cobalt from cutting and polishing results in hard metal disease (HMD). [17] Occupational asthma is also commonly associated with cobalt exposure alone or in the setting of tungsten carbide. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Machine -- Function -- Carbon Dioxide Absorbent
retrieved_docs.content: An essential component of the circle system breathing circuit is the carbon dioxide (CO2) absorbent. CO2 absorbent contains various mixtures of calcium hydroxide, sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, and barium hydroxide to prevent the introduction of carbon dioxide into the inspiratory limb of the anesthesia machine. [6] Expired gases pass through the filter where carbon dioxide undergoes chemical reactions with these respective bases to be trapped in the filter to allow for safer rebreathing of the expired air. The filtered expired air allows for expired gases to be recycled, allowing for low-flow anesthesia (gas flows less than alveolar ventilation to minimize costs of providing anesthesia). CO2 absorbents used in anesthesia machines typically have chemical indicators that change color as the filter becomes saturated. When the filter is two-thirds saturated, the filter should be changed to prevent the re-breathing of carbon dioxide. Modern anesthesia machine monitors will often indicate when carbon dioxide is detected in the inspiratory limb, further alerting the anesthesiologist that the soda lime filter needs to be exchanged. Carbon dioxide becomes present in the inspiratory limb when the carbon dioxide absorbent fails to absorb the carbon dioxide in the expired gases present in the expiratory limb. [1] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Carbon Dioxide Detector -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Their location in the airway circuit is also used to categorize detectors. These are typically divided into mainstream and sidestream devices. Mainstream detectors utilize an IR sensor positioned directly within the gas flow path and produce real-time graphs of the CO 2 waveform. These tend to be more costly units used primarily for intubated patients. Sidestream detectors are located off the main path of flow in the airway circuit. Sidestream detectors often use small-diameter tubing leading to a detector that allows gas sampling. Nasal monitoring of end-tidal CO 2 typically makes use of this configuration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmechanical method of resuscitation encompasses manual techniques for reviving patients, and its specific child concept is إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), which represents the hands‑on approach used to restore breathing and circulation without reliance on mechanical devices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology includes the concept of respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation, which are essential airway management interventions used to secure the airway and provide assisted breathing in critically ill patients. It also incorporates the concept of thoracoscopic wedge resection of the lung, a minimally invasive surgical procedure performed to remove a small, wedge‑shaped portion of lung tissue for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. By encompassing both of these procedures, the CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology provides a standardized framework for capturing and reporting these distinct clinical interventions in clinical trial data, facilitating consistent documentation and analysis across studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2919
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of intraluminal foreign body from bronchus by bronchoscopy is performed using a specific removal technique, and nonoperative removal of foreign body likewise employs a removal technique. Both procedures are categorized under removal techniques for foreign bodies, highlighting minimally invasive methods for extracting intraluminal objects from the airway without surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2144

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Bronchoscopy with removal of foreign body, endoscopic removal of intraluminal foreign body from the trachea, and nonoperative removal of foreign body are all procedures that employ specific removal techniques. These removal techniques provide minimally invasive, non‑surgical methods for extracting foreign objects from the airway, helping to restore patency while reducing patient morbidity and the need for operative intervention.
